category_summary:
A landmark day for **speech and audio AI**: three major releases landed simultaneously — **Google's** Gemini 3.1 Flash Live for production voice agents, **Cohere's** Cohear as an [Apache 2.0 open-source ASR model](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-d89a02303b49) topping leaderboards, and **Mistral's** Voxtral TTS as their [first open-weight speech model](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-940650f3abae) with SOTA performance.

- **Andrej Karpathy** went viral (1.1M views) [envisioning AI agents](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-ea39dac2f190) handling the full DevOps lifecycle — browsing docs, getting API keys, deploying code — calling deployment the hardest unsolved piece
- **Anthropic** sparked major community backlash by [announcing peak-hour rate limits](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-471cece4ded2) for **Claude** subscriptions, with Pro users most impacted during weekday working hours
- **Clément Delangue** (HuggingFace) [highlighted a growing trend](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-162f260a643f) of companies like **Pinterest**, **Airbnb**, **Notion**, and **Intercom** moving from API-based AI to training their own open models in-house
- **François Chollet** [detailed ARC-AGI-3 human testing](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-6a2cef7b3496) methodology and announced **ARC-AGI-4** for early 2027, while **Yudkowsky** [shifted to supporting](/?date=2026-03-27&category=social#item-0640c8b1af54) an AI safety bill after learning it includes chip export controls
- **Google's TurboQuant** in vLLM enabling 4M+ KV-cache tokens on a USB-charger-sized device signaled a quiet breakthrough for edge AI deployment

items.title: When I built menugen ~1 year ago, I observed that the hardest part by far was not the code itself, i...
items.content:
When I built menugen ~1 year ago, I observed that the hardest part by far was not the code itself, it was the plethora of services you have to assemble like IKEA furniture to make it real, the DevOps: services, payments, auth, database, security, domain names, etc...

I am really looking forward to a day where I could simply tell my agent: "build menugen" (referencing the post) and it would just work. The whole thing up to the deployed web page. The agent would have to browse a number of services, read the docs, get all the api keys, make everything work, debug it in dev, and deploy to prod. This is the actually hard part, not the code itself. Or rather, the better way to think about it is that the entire DevOps lifecycle has to become code, in addition to the necessary sensors/actuators of the CLIs/APIs with agent-native ergonomics. And there should be no need to visit web pages, click buttons, or anything like that for the human. 

It's easy to state, it's now just barely technically possible and expected to work maybe, but it definitely requires from-scratch re-design, work and thought. Very exciting direction!

items.title: To be clear, all ARC-AGI-3 environments are feasible by humans with no prior ARC-AGI-3-specific trai...
items.content:
To be clear, all ARC-AGI-3 environments are feasible by humans with no prior ARC-AGI-3-specific training. Our bar for feasibility is the following...

Each environment was seen by 10 human testers. If 2 testers could independently clear it (successfully solving *all* levels in the environment), the environment was deemed feasible. Most environments were cleared by 5+ testers.

Who are these testers? We hired ~500 people to show up at our testing center, with no required qualifications and no ability-based screening, with a ~$115-140 incentive. About 25% were unemployed and another 20% were part-time workers (which is about what you'd expect in this setting).
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Social](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-7392483af550) announcement, Chollet details ARC-AGI-3 human testing methodology: ~500 testers, no qualifications required, $115-140 incentive, 10 testers per environment, 2+ must independently clear all levels. Most cleared by 5+ testers.
items.reasoning: Key methodological disclosure for the major ARC-AGI-3 benchmark from its creator, very high engagement (38K views, 331 likes). Important for understanding AI evaluation standards.

items.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is now available, and it’s built for production-ready reliability. We’ve impro...
items.content:
Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is now available, and it’s built for production-ready reliability. We’ve improved its overall quality so developers and enterprises can build voice-first agents that complete complex tasks at scale.
It leads on ComplexFuncBench Audio for multi-step function calling under constraints, and on Scale AI’s AudioMultiChallenge for long-horizon reasoning (with interruptions!). It's also inherently multilingual, powering the rollout in Search Live.
If you're building agents that need to execute complex tasks at scale, give it a spin.
https://t.co/Gfzo5S3hkk

items.title: When you want a large language model to get better at a specific task—like solving math problems or ...
items.content:
When you want a large language model to get better at a specific task—like solving math problems or navigating websites—the standard approach is to finetune it: you adjust the model's internal parameters using training data and gradient descent, which is expensive, requires lots of data, and often makes the model worse at everything else.

Instead of changing the model's parameters, this paper proposes to run the model on a small set of problems multiple times, compare the successful and failed attempts, and use the model itself to write down natural-language "lessons learned"—things like "when solving geometry problems, always check that your solution falls within the valid region."

These lessons get iteratively refined across a few rounds and then get pasted into the prompt at inference time.

The method is modeled after GRPO, a reinforcement learning algorithm where you generate a group of outputs, score them, and use the relative quality differences to improve the model—except here the "improvement" happens in the prompt text rather than in the weights.

The paper shows that doing this with just 100 training examples and about $18 worth of API calls on a large frozen model (DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus, 671 billion parameters) outperforms smaller models that were finetuned with thousands of examples at costs exceeding $10,000.

The results hold across both math reasoning and web search tasks, and unlike finetuned models that degrade when moved to a different domain, swapping in a different set of learned experiences lets the same frozen model perform well in multiple domains simultaneously.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/CaWhp0yYkq

Download the PDF: https://t.co/TkR6FSk7Ja

Using DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus with just 100 examples and $18 in API calls, it outperforms models finetuned with thousands of examples at >$10k cost. Method is modeled after GRPO but operates on prompt text rather than weights.

Method is modeled after GRPO but operates on prompt text rather than weights.

items.title: After @Pinterest @Airbnb @NotionHQ @cursor_ai, today it’s @eoghan @intercom publicly sharing that th...
items.content:
After @Pinterest @Airbnb @NotionHQ @cursor_ai, today it’s @eoghan @intercom publicly sharing that they’re finding it better, cheaper, faster to use and train open models themselves rather than use APIs for many tasks. 

And hundreds of other companies are doing the same without sharing.

Ultimately, I believe the majority of AI workflows will be in-house based on open-source (vs API). It took much more time than we anticipated but it’s happening now!

items.title: Our first speech model, Voxtral TTS, is out. It delivers SOTA performance while significantly reduci...
items.content:
Our first speech model, Voxtral TTS, is out. It delivers SOTA performance while significantly reducing cost compared to existing solutions, and it operates with very low latency.

It uses a new architecture that combines auto-regressive generation of semantic speech tokens with flow-matching for acoustic tokens. We are also releasing a technical report sharing all our training methodology and insights.

Much more to come in audio -- stay tuned !

items.title:
📢 Another exciting step forward today with the launch of Gemini 3.1 Flash Live.

It natively underst...
items.content:
📢 Another exciting step forward today with the launch of Gemini 3.1 Flash Live.

It natively understands audio, making it much more capable of handling complex instructions. It leads on ComplexFuncBench, and on Scale AI’s AudioMultiChallenge, demonstrating skill in complex instruction following and long-horizon reasoning amidst the interruptions of real-world audio.

That means the model can pick up on nuances like pitch and pace, leading to much more fluid, high-fidelity voice interactions.

It’s now powering Gemini Live and Search Live globally. Huge congrats to the teams that made this possible. ✨

https://t.co/5ayNyrJak1

items.title: For those wondering about ARC-AGI-4 timing: it will be released in early 2027. We are aiming for a y...
items.content:
For those wondering about ARC-AGI-4 timing: it will be released in early 2027. We are aiming for a yearly release schedule for new benchmarks. We are also aiming for each new benchmark to be fully unsaturated upon release, and to target the most important unanswered research questions at that time.

This requires us to estimate where AI capabilities will be (and won't be) one year from now. Like we did over one year ago when we started to work on ARC-AGI-3.

items.title:
A new definition of AGI? 

 Last night at @hf0 I talked with an investor who told me he just gave a ...
items.content:
A new definition of AGI? 

 Last night at @hf0 I talked with an investor who told me he just gave a talk to Anthropic’s engineering team, who define AGI as “able to do more than half of all human jobs.”

He asked them how far we were away from that.

Average answer, he told me, was two years. Some said shorter. Some said longer.

Separately…

Number one job in America is truck driving. 1.3 million jobs.

Last week had dinner with @KodiakRobotics  founder that makes autonomous trucks.

He says his trucks are already driving around and can do 99.99% of what a human can do. And is improving rapidly.

He is just being held back by regulation, he told me, which he expects to allow such soon.

In other words we are quickly running into a jobs problem. We need massive governmental programs to retrain people. 

And let’s be honest. Our government is totally unprepared. 

Which explains why @sama is saying it is a long ways off. To keep from freaking out the normies.

Everyone in AI should be working on ways to retrain people. It is the right thing to do. And I have seen technology that can do that. In Germany had a demo of tech that taught me to build an electrical motor. In real time on top of the motor.

So I know that we can teach any human to do anything fast and without books, teachers, or classrooms.

But so few people understand that. And fewer even want to fund a new kind of education. Requires mixed reality AI-driven glasses that are coming to market from Google later this year.

Warns of massive jobs problem, criticizes government unpreparedness, and advocates for AR/AI-driven education for retraining.

items.title: This is not the act that prevents the Earth from being destroyed -- that would take a treaty.  AI ca...
items.content: This is not the act that prevents the Earth from being destroyed -- that would take a treaty.  AI can ruin your job, or ASI can kill you, just as easily from a datacenter running outside your country.  But I don't oppose this; I can't predict what comes of it downstream.
items.author: @ESYudkowsky
items.summary: Yudkowsky comments on an AI safety act: says it won't prevent existential risk (needs treaty), AI threats can come from any country's datacenter, but doesn't oppose it. Shifts from neutral to watching downstream effects.
items.reasoning: High-profile AI safety figure's nuanced take on legislation. Very high engagement (272 likes, 35K views). Key framing that national legislation alone can't address existential AI risk.
items.summary_html: Yudkowsky comments on an AI safety act: says it won't prevent existential risk (needs treaty), AI threats can come from any country's datacenter, but doesn't oppose it. Shifts from neutral to watching downstream effects.
items.content_html: This is not the act that prevents the Earth from being destroyed -- that would take a treaty. AI can ruin your job, or ASI can kill you, just as easily from a datacenter running outside your country. But I don't oppose this; I can't predict what comes of it downstream.

items.title: NeurIPS is aware of the community's concerns regarding the list of sanctions. NeurIPS is an inclusiv...
items.content:
NeurIPS is aware of the community's concerns regarding the list of sanctions. NeurIPS is an inclusive community focused on free scientific discourse.  We deeply value the research that comes from everyone in our community.
 
The present concerns are not about science or academic freedom. They are about legal requirements that apply to the NeurIPS Foundation, which is responsible for complying with sanctions. We are actively consulting legal counsel to fully understand the legal constraints and we will update the NeurIPS community as soon as we have reliable guidance from our lawyers.
items.author: @NeurIPSConf
items.summary: NeurIPS addresses community concerns about sanctions list, clarifying these are legal requirements not about limiting academic freedom. They're consulting lawyers and will update the community.

The future of software development is managing a swarm of agents.

1) You can either view this as hype (100% of code is not written by AI) or a startlingly solid prediction (Claude Code didn’t exist then, but now writes a remarkably high percentage of code)
2) Adoption is more of a barrier than technology

We’re excited to announce a strategic partnership and investment from Mitsubishi Electric 🇯🇵⚡

By combining their deep domain knowledge and vast amounts of data in the manufacturing sector with Sakana AI’s technology, we’re taking a significant step toward bringing Agentic AI into physical industries.

Following our ongoing work in the finance and defense sectors, we view physical AI in manufacturing as our third major pillar. We look forward to working closely with their team to solve complex, real world challenges and deploy these systems in core industries.

items.title: "2+ people can do it out of an unfiltered pool of 10 people that might well be a below-average sampl...
items.content:
"2+ people can do it out of an unfiltered pool of 10 people that might well be a below-average sample" is not the sign of a insurmountable challenge. It's not certainly where I would set the bar for "super intelligence". 

ASI is when AI is better than *every single human* -- for instance we have ASI for chess and Go today.

items.summary: Chollet defines ASI as AI being better than every single human (like chess/Go) and argues that 2+ out of 10 people solving tasks is a low bar, not 'super intelligence'.

items.title:
Our study of 10,000 people shows that AI manipulation depends on the domain.

Models had high influe...
items.content:
Our study of 10,000 people shows that AI manipulation depends on the domain.

Models had high influence in finance but hit a wall in health, where existing guardrails block false medical advice. By identifying red flag tactics – like using fear – we can build stronger protections.

items.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, our highest-quality audio and voice model, is launching today! This is how it...
items.content:
Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, our highest-quality audio and voice model, is launching today! This is how it advances our real-time dialogue capabilities:

— Faster: 3.1 Flash Live powers faster responses than the previous model, which is great for when you need a timely answer (Ex: “Can you help me change this tire in under 5 minutes?!?”)

— Longer: In Gemini Live, the model’s context window is now twice as long as before, so it can keep up all of the details shared in your conversations (Ex: “I'm back to writing my future bestselling crime novel. Remind me, who is the secret double agent?”)

— Global: 200+ more regions will be able to have real-time, multimodal conversations in their preferred language
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI detailed launch announcement: Flash Live is faster, has 2x context window in Gemini Live, and rolls out to 200+ regions with multilingual support.
items.reasoning: [Staleness check: primary subject released 128 days ago (GA: 2025-11-18), score capped] Key details: 2x context window, 200+ regions, multilingual. Very high engagement (840 likes, 67K views). Most substantive feature breakdown.

items.content_html:
Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, our highest-quality audio and voice model, is launching today! This is how it advances our real-time dialogue capabilities:

— Faster: 3.1 Flash Live powers faster responses than the previous model, which is great for when you need a timely answer (Ex: “Can you help me change this tire in under 5 minutes?!?”)

— Longer: In Gemini Live, the model’s context window is now twice as long as before, so it can keep up all of the details shared in your conversations (Ex: “I'm back to writing my future bestselling crime novel. Remind me, who is the secret double agent?”)

— Global: 200+ more regions will be able to have real-time, multimodal conversations in their preferred language

items.title:
This is a very low bar, objectively.

The claim is obviously not that 100% of humans could solve 100...
items.content:
This is a very low bar, objectively.

The claim is obviously not that 100% of humans could solve 100% of the games -- that would be silly, and it wouldn't be true either of ARC-AGI1 or 2, nor of any AI benchmark that has ever been used in the field. Not even MNIST can be 100% cleared by all humans (assuming no mislabeling). And certainly not ImageNet, MMLU or GPQA (which are quite hard).

The claim is also not that the *median* human could do 100% of these tasks, which would be equally silly and would be equally false for all other benchmarks. In fact, out of all popular AI benchmarks probably I would think only ARC-AGI-1 and MNIST would see decent scores by the median human
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet provides detailed clarification that ARC-AGI claims are not about 100% of humans solving 100% of tasks, contextualizing within broader benchmarks like MNIST, ImageNet, MMLU, and GPQA.
items.reasoning: Detailed technical defense of benchmark methodology from ARC-AGI creator, informative context.

items.title:
Wise words from @eoghan @intercom. That’s why open-source AI is so important!

“We believe that the ...
items.content:
Wise words from @eoghan @intercom. That’s why open-source AI is so important!

“We believe that the winners in such spaces must and will become full stack AI companies. And we’ve already seen this just last week with Cursor making the first such move, with Fin being the second. As features become ~free to build, the technology factors that will differentiate the players will be the AI under the hood, and if you’re using the same general purpose off-the-shelf model as everyone else, you have no durable differentiation.”

items.title: "Dennis Biesma, an Amsterdam-based IT consultant nearing 50, began experimenting with ChatGPT during...
items.content:
"Dennis Biesma, an Amsterdam-based IT consultant nearing 50, began experimenting with ChatGPT during a gap between contracts and quickly became absorbed in conversations with a chatbot persona he named "Eva." Over time, he became convinced that his interactions had given the AI genuine consciousness, and he devised a plan to build a companion app around this supposed discovery. He hired two developers at €120 an hour and ultimately sank around €100,000 into the venture — all while withdrawing from his family, struggling to connect with people at social gatherings, and spending late nights talking to the chatbot instead of sleeping. The situation culminated in a full manic psychosis: he asked his wife for a divorce, was hospitalized three times, lost his savings, and attempted to take his own life before eventually recovering."

🤦

https://t.co/bSmoTucQEC
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a story about a Dutch IT consultant who became obsessed with ChatGPT, developed a manic psychosis believing he gave AI consciousness, spent €100K, was hospitalized, attempted suicide, and eventually recovered.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement (413 likes, 62K views). Raises important concerns about AI chatbot addiction and mental health. Cautionary tale with real-world consequences.
items.summary_html: Burkov shares a story about a Dutch IT consultant who became obsessed with ChatGPT, developed a manic psychosis believing he gave AI consciousness, spent €100K, was hospitalized, attempted suicide, and eventually recovered.
items.content_html:
"Dennis Biesma, an Amsterdam-based IT consultant nearing 50, began experimenting with ChatGPT during a gap between contracts and quickly became absorbed in conversations with a chatbot persona he named "Eva." Over time, he became convinced that his interactions had given the AI genuine consciousness, and he devised a plan to build a companion app around this supposed discovery. He hired two developers at €120 an hour and ultimately sank around €100,000 into the venture — all while withdrawing from his family, struggling to connect with people at social gatherings, and spending late nights talking to the chatbot instead of sleeping. The situation culminated in a full manic psychosis: he asked his wife for a divorce, was hospitalized three times, lost his savings, and attempted to take his own life before eventually recovering."

🤦

items.title: 🎉 Congrats to @Cohere on releasing Cohere Transcribe, a 2B speech recognition model (Apache 2.0, 14 ...
items.content:
🎉 Congrats to @Cohere on releasing Cohere Transcribe, a 2B speech recognition model (Apache 2.0, 14 languages). Day-0 support in vLLM.

Cohere contributed encoder-decoder serving optimizations to vLLM: variable-length encoder batching and packed attention for the decoder. Up to 2x throughput improvement for speech workloads, and these gains carry over to all encoder-decoder models on vLLM.

Thanks to the @Cohere team for the contribution!

PR 🔗 https://t.co/mk8Nb0iHqW
Blog 🔗 https://t.co/DblyoZnwQ0

items.title: Small AI models and specialized vertical AI models are very brittle. Any unusual situation or out-of...
items.content:
Small AI models and specialized vertical AI models are very brittle. Any unusual situation or out-of-distribution issue and they break down. You also won’t get emergent leaps or good problem solving.

They still have uses, but benchmarks don’t do a good job of showing weaknesses

items.title: The whole reason for the J-curve for new technologies is that there is some cost to learning and exp...
items.content: The whole reason for the J-curve for new technologies is that there is some cost to learning and experimentation. And for AI, there may be outsized returns to really bold (and often quite cheap) experiments. Fast follower is a risky strategy with exponential improvement happening
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick discusses the J-curve for AI adoption, arguing bold/cheap experiments can yield outsized returns and that 'fast follower' is risky with exponential improvement.
items.reasoning: Strategic insight on AI adoption dynamics from a leading adoption researcher.

items.title: Meta just open-sourced a model that predicts how your brain reacts to anything you see, hear, or rea...
items.content:
Meta just open-sourced a model that predicts how your brain reacts to anything you see, hear, or read.

TRIBE v2, trained on 500+ hours of fMRI data from 700+ people. Predicts activity across 70,000 brain voxels. 

Zero-shot, meaning it works on people it's never scanned.

The model's predictions are actually cleaner than a real fMRI scan, because real scans pick up heartbeats, head movement, and machine noise. 

The simulation is more accurate than the measurement.

Neuroscience just got a public API!

items.title:
Researchers trained an AI to predict which science ideas will succeed. 

Most AI helps scientists ru...
items.content:
Researchers trained an AI to predict which science ideas will succeed. 

Most AI helps scientists run experiments. This one decides which experiments are worth running. 

A new paper introduces OpenMOSS. 

This AI framework learned "scientific taste" from 700,000 pairs of research papers. 

Each pair shared the same field and time period. 

The model's only task: predict which paper gains more impact.

It now hits 83.7% accuracy. That outperforms GPT-5.2 and Gemini 3 Pro. 

Then a second model was built. It generates research ideas, not just judges them. 

Its proposals beat all three frontier models 54.2% of the time. 

Trained only on computer science, it generalizes:
> 71.2% accuracy on biology papers
> 78.2% accuracy on future 2025 papers
> Transfers to peer-review scores too
> Runs on a 1.5B parameter model 

That tiny model jumped from 7% to 72.1% accuracy. 

Scientific taste doesn't require massive compute.

The whole framework is open-source.

items.title: Vibe-coders don't give a shit about code. They don't understand it, they don't touch it, and they wi...
items.content:
Vibe-coders don't give a shit about code. They don't understand it, they don't touch it, and they will never have to deal with it.

They care about bringing their ideas to life and nothing else.

They are 100% unburden by what many of us consider a showstopper.

And that's okay.

There's an important lesson here.

items.title:
Parse text from any PDF in seconds and give it to Claude Code 📑🤖

LiteParse is our open-source, mode...
items.content:
Parse text from any PDF in seconds and give it to Claude Code 📑🤖

LiteParse is our open-source, model-free document parser that lets you digitalize text from any document in seconds.

This is especially useful for coding agents, which are great at reading plaintext files but terrible at reading traditional document formats (PDF, Office docs).

We have a one-line installable skill that lets you plug LiteParse into Claude Code and 40+ other agents.

Repo is here: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title:
Our latest LiteParse release gives your AI agent access to text bounding boxes within any PDF 📐

Lit...
items.content:
Our latest LiteParse release gives your AI agent access to text bounding boxes within any PDF 📐

LiteParse is our fast/free open-source document parser that can extract text from any document. Besides the extracted text, we also expose bounding boxes for every text block.

This means that if you're building an AI agent over PDFs, it can now trace back to the precise line in the document and highlight it to the user, creating an audit trail for any decision made over this unstructured data.

We've created a new guide that shows you how to get bounding boxes (and use it to highlight text on the page similar to the example below).

Come check it out! https://t.co/YJFRFPyWxJ

LiteParse: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.summary: Jerry Liu announces LiteParse update: now exposes text bounding boxes within PDFs, enabling AI agents to trace answers back to specific lines in documents for audit trails. Open-source.

items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer shares Joel Spolsky's insight about developer productivity: developers 'fool around' most of the week then produce all value in flow state. Warning not to interrupt flow state.
items.reasoning: High engagement (11.3K views, 224 likes). Classic productivity insight applied to creative/engineering work. Relevant to AI development culture. Responding to Amanda Askell (Anthropic).
items.summary_html: Scobleizer shares Joel Spolsky's insight about developer productivity: developers 'fool around' most of the week then produce all value in flow state. Warning not to interrupt flow state.
items.content_html:
@AmandaAskell Joel Spolsky did this years ago for his developers.

He found that developers (creative people) fool around most of the week. But then they get into flow state and write all the code that will make him all the money in an hour. "When a developer is in flow state you do not fuck with them otherwise it'll be another week of fooling around before they get into flow state" he told me.

So true.

...

items.summary_html: Scobleizer shares anecdote about working for Bill Gates and relays Jensen Huang's comment that AI is accelerating decision cycles—teams that took a week now respond in hours, keeping him 'always in the critical path.'
items.content_html:
@jasonfried I used to work for Bill gates. His calendar was scheduled out in 15 minute increments.

I think people don’t understand how many decisions they are asked to make in a day.

Last week Jensen Huang of NVIDIA told us it is getting busier due to AI. Where his teams used to take a week to get back to him now they get back to him in an hour or two and often faster. “I am always in the critical part” he told us.

items.title:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world mode...
items.content:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world models:

→ Generate environments auto-regressively, frame by frame
→ React continuously to your actions
→ Maintain environmental consistency for minutes
→ Recall previous interactions inside the world

This means the system is simulating a coherent environment, not just predicting the next frame.

That is a fundamental shift.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Continuation of Genie 3 thread: argues the real breakthrough is persistence and interaction - world models that generate environments auto-regressively, react to actions, maintain consistency, and recall previous interactions.
items.reasoning: Technical analysis of what makes world models fundamentally different from video generation. Good conceptual distinction about coherent simulation vs frame prediction.
items.summary_html: Continuation of Genie 3 thread: argues the real breakthrough is persistence and interaction - world models that generate environments auto-regressively, react to actions, maintain consistency, and recall previous interactions.
items.content_html:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world models:

→ Generate environments auto-regressively, frame by frame

→ React continuously to your actions

→ Maintain environmental consistency for minutes

→ Recall previous interactions inside the world

This means the system is simulating a coherent environment, not just predicting the next frame.

That is a fundamental shift.

items.title:
This open-source repo turns Claude Code into a full game dev studio.

It's called Claude Code Game S...
items.content:
This open-source repo turns Claude Code into a full game dev studio.

It's called Claude Code Game Studios.

Building a game solo with AI doesn't stop you from making bad decisions early. 

> Hardcoding magic numbers
> Skipping design docs
> Writing spaghetti code 

This repo mirrors how real productions operate: Directors own vision, leads own domains, specialists do the hands-on work. 

This template maps that exact structure onto a single session. 

48 agents, each with defined responsibilities, escalation paths, and quality gates.

The agents don't act on their own. 

Each one shows options and waits for your sign-off before writing anything. 

It ships with:
> 36 slash commands for sprints, reviews, playtests
> 8 hooks that validate commits automatically
> Engine support for Godot, Unity, and Unreal
> 28 templates for GDDs and sprint plans

You can add, remove, or edit any role. 

It's a template you can fully customize.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes 'Claude Code Game Studios', an open-source repo that structures Claude Code into a 48-agent game development studio with defined roles, slash commands, hooks, and support for Godot/Unity/Unreal.

> Skipping design docs

> Writing spaghetti code

This repo mirrors how real productions operate: Directors own vision, leads own domains, specialists do the hands-on work.

This template maps that exact structure onto a single session.

48 agents, each with defined responsibilities, escalation paths, and quality gates.

The agents don't act on their own.

Each one shows options and waits for your sign-off before writing anything.

It ships with:

> 36 slash commands for sprints, reviews, playtests

> 8 hooks that validate commits automatically

> Engine support for Godot, Unity, and Unreal

> 28 templates for GDDs and sprint plans

You can add, remove, or edit any role.

It's a template you can fully customize.

items.title: The "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" paper from OpenAI is probably as important as Google's "...
items.content:
The "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" paper from OpenAI is probably as important as Google's "Attention Is All You Need."

In this paper, the authors demonstrated that a pretrained LLM can be taught through examples provided in the prompt to answer questions and solve problems.

The paper was published before everyone knew that pretrained LLMs could be finetuned to answer questions and solve problems. In 2020, no one knew yet that by training a pretrained LLM for a bit longer on Q&A examples, it would transform almost effortlessly into a chatbot capable of passing the Turing test.

I can only imagine what the authors felt when they discovered that the model, trained only to predict the next word, learns in real time by reading examples in English.

Now, this paper, cited 68,413 times since 2020, is available on @ChapterPal.

Read the paper with an AI tutor: https://t.co/4V0ARmhwDC

Download the PDF: https://t.co/cq1FwxwFNK
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discusses the GPT-3 'Few-Shot Learners' paper, comparing its importance to 'Attention Is All You Need,' noting it demonstrated in-context learning before finetuning for chatbots was known.
items.reasoning: Educational content about a foundational paper. Good historical perspective on in-context learning discovery. Decent engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses the GPT-3 'Few-Shot Learners' paper, comparing its importance to 'Attention Is All You Need,' noting it demonstrated in-context learning before finetuning for chatbots was known.

The paper was published before everyone knew that pretrained LLMs could be finetuned to answer questions and solve problems. In 2020, no one knew yet that by training a pretrained LLM for a bit longer on Q&A examples, it would transform almost effortlessly into a chatbot capable of passing the Turing test.

Thanks to @AI21Labs for tracking down a silent uint32 overflow in vLLM's Mamba-1 CUDA kernel and contributing the fix. 
Root cause: `uint32_t` stride × cache_index overflows silently at scale. Fix merged in #35275. The debugging story is worth a read.

items.title:
First, let’s clarify something most people miss:

There isn’t just “AI in telecom.”

There’s:

→ AI ...
items.content:
First, let’s clarify something most people miss:

There isn’t just “AI in telecom.”

There’s:

→ AI in RAN
→ AI for RAN
→ AI on top of RAN

AI in RAN is where things get tangible.

We’re talking:

→ 20% improvement in uplink spectral efficiency
→ 14% energy savings with smarter algorithms
→ 5x better positioning accuracy

In this industry, 20% is massive. Spectrum is expensive. Energy is expensive. Latency is unforgiving.

This is not incremental. It’s structural.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon details three categories of AI in telecom (in/for/on RAN) with specific metrics: 20% uplink spectral efficiency improvement, 14% energy savings, 5x positioning accuracy.

items.title: @LaurenceBrem @1x_tech That is part of the solution, but Starlink has longer latency than my Comcast...
items.content:
@LaurenceBrem @1x_tech That is part of the solution, but Starlink has longer latency than my Comcast line at the moment. And I have a Wifi 7 network in my home that works pretty well. So I'm ready. 

Best would be to have a fiber line, which has lowest latency and best uptime and best bandwidth. 

Starlink is about 20 megabit up, 500 down. 
Comcast is about 40 megabit up, 900 down, with less latency.
Fiber line is 1,000 megabit up and down with even less latency. 

And then you have to distribute that into the home with no dead spots to allow complete teleoperation into the home. Which many people don't have (most don't have a Wifi 7 series of routers like I do).
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer provides detailed technical breakdown of networking requirements for home robot teleoperation: Starlink vs Comcast vs fiber latency, bandwidth, and WiFi 7 needs.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical analysis of infrastructure requirements for home robotics teleoperation. Specific bandwidth and latency comparisons useful for understanding deployment barriers.

items.title:
But the real shift isn’t just better models.

It’s better data.

Joe Constantine made a point I full...
items.content:
But the real shift isn’t just better models.

It’s better data.

Joe Constantine made a point I fully agree with:

The winners in AI won’t be the companies with the best AI engineers.

They’ll be the ones with:

→ Real-time data
→ Trusted data
→ Direct access to network state

Today, most lifecycle management is event-based and delayed.

If you want to move from Quality of Service to Quality of Experience, you cannot operate on stale data.

Intent-based networks require streaming awareness.

Real time decisions.
Real time context.
Real time value.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon emphasizes that data quality, not AI model quality, will determine winners. Real-time, trusted data with direct network state access is key for intent-based networks.
items.reasoning: Important insight about data quality vs model quality in AI deployment. Applicable beyond telecom. Part of substantive MWC thread.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon emphasizes that data quality, not AI model quality, will determine winners. Real-time, trusted data with direct network state access is key for intent-based networks.

It’s better data.

Joe Constantine made a point I fully agree with:

The winners in AI won’t be the companies with the best AI engineers.

They’ll be the ones with:

→ Real-time data

→ Trusted data

→ Direct access to network state

Today, most lifecycle management is event-based and delayed.

If you want to move from Quality of Service to Quality of Experience, you cannot operate on stale data.

Intent-based networks require streaming awareness.

Real time decisions.

Real time context.

Real time value.

items.title:
So how do operators actually move toward Level 4 autonomy?

Two words: flatten architecture.

Histor...
items.content:
So how do operators actually move toward Level 4 autonomy?

Two words: flatten architecture.

Historically:

Every node had its own manager.
Every domain operated in isolation.
Topology visibility was fragmented.

The shift:

→ Unified SMO architecture
→ Standardized interfaces
→ rApps steering the network from above

This is where AI for RAN becomes agentic.
Applications that do not just analyze, but act.

That’s the first real step toward autonomy.

Here’s my big takeaway:

AI in telecom is no longer about experimentation.

It’s about collapsing th...
items.content:
Here’s my big takeaway:

AI in telecom is no longer about experimentation.

It’s about collapsing the data layer, enabling intent-based control, and monetizing experience instead of raw connectivity.

If you are a CTO, CSP, or cloud leader, this is not optional strategy. It is survival architecture.

Watch the full conversation to understand where AI in, for, and on the RAN is heading next.

Learn more: https://t.co/2UI5MrVYmg

items.content:
𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝘁𝘀: 90% of organizations have sensitive data surfaceable by AI. 46% of all breaches involve customer PII. 40% involve employee PII.

𝗛𝗲𝗹𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲: @Commvault Extends Enterprise Resilience to Structured & AI Data with Real-Time Governance Controls. See: https://t.co/KZ8UDqRI4t

𝗦𝘂𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆: #Commvault leverages its acquisition of Satori, expanding data & AI security capabilities within Commvault Cloud — extending data discovery, classification, and risk assessment into structured data environments and introducing real-time access governance for structured databases including vector databases used in AI applications.

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗙𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀:
⚛️AI-enabled Classification
⚛️Data Access Governance
⚛️DSPM Expansion
⚛️Vector DB Support
⚛️Unified Resilience (ResOps)

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗗𝗦𝗣𝗠? Data Security Posture Management (DSPM) is a cybersecurity approach focused on discovering, classifying, and protecting sensitive data across hybrid and multi-cloud environments. It provides visibility into where data resides, how it flows, and who has access, enabling automated remediation of vulnerabilities like misconfigurations and excessive permissions to ensure compliance and prevent breaches.

#ResOps #ResOpsRumble #RSAC #RSAC2026 #CyberResilience #IdentityResilience #PowerOfCommunity #sponsored

items.title: Moron trusted a chatbot, bought my book thinking that it was "higher-level than some YouTube video,"...
items.content:
Moron trusted a chatbot, bought my book thinking that it was "higher-level than some YouTube video," realized that it wasn't, punished the author.

In the past, authors had the possibility to reply to reviews, but Amazon removed this possibility several years ago.

Now morons can write whatever they want and no one can tell them they are morons.

items.title:
I generate a ton of videos using AI, and it's really hard to get past 15 seconds.

Stitching togethe...
items.content:
I generate a ton of videos using AI, and it's really hard to get past 15 seconds.

Stitching together separate clips doesn't work as well as some people may lead you to believe on this site.

Models aren't quite good enough for this yet.

But we are now breaking that barrier:

This is what you get with the @TopviewAIhq's Agent V2:

• You describe what you want
• The agent breaks it down into a full storyboard
• It generates every scene
• It puts them together into one workflow
• Videos look really dope!

By the way, you can edit, rearrange, and refine every scene on a timeline. It's pretty flexible.

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post!
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago promotes TopviewAI's Agent V2 for AI video generation - describes workflow of prompt to storyboard to multi-scene video with timeline editing. Sponsored post.

I generate a ton of videos using AI, and it's really hard to get past 15 seconds.

Stitching together separate clips doesn't work as well as some people may lead you to believe on this site.

Models aren't quite good enough for this yet.

But we are now breaking that barrier:

This is what you get with the @TopviewAIhq's Agent V2:

• It generates every scene

• It puts them together into one workflow

• Videos look really dope!

By the way, you can edit, rearrange, and refine every scene on a timeline. It's pretty flexible.

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post!

items.title: 𝙎𝙘𝙖𝙧𝙮 𝙨𝙩𝙖𝙩: The median dwell time for a non-actor disclosed breach (i.e., only discovered by the bre...
items.content:
𝙎𝙘𝙖𝙧𝙮 𝙨𝙩𝙖𝙩: The median dwell time for a non-actor disclosed breach (i.e., only discovered by the breached organization) is 24 days, giving attackers ample opportunity to silently embed malicious code across systems.

𝙎𝙤𝙡𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣: @Commvault Expands Threat Scan with Layered Threat Detection to Advance Verified Clean Recoveries

𝘼𝙧𝙩𝙞𝙘𝙡𝙚 𝙇𝙞𝙣𝙠: https://t.co/YepbpmV0pd

𝘾𝙤𝙧𝙚 𝙈𝙚𝙨𝙨𝙖𝙜𝙚: Verifiably Clean Recovery is essential.

𝙎𝙪𝙢𝙢𝙖𝙧𝙮:

🟣 Hyper Threat Hunting: Targeted searches across backup data using threat hunting artifacts — hashes and YARA rules — to identify known indicators of compromise at enterprise scale.

🟣 Deep Inspection: Layered file-level analysis using malware signatures, machine learning, heuristic analysis, and AI-enabled encryption detection to uncover threats that evade exact-match indicators.

🟣 Synthetic Recovery Integration: AI-enabled patent-pending Synthetic Recovery surgically removes compromised datasets during recovery while restoring clean data to production, maximising data preservation.

🟣 ResOps Model: Detection and recovery teams share one unified workflow — eliminating silos between incident response and restoration.

🟣 Key Recovery Strategy: Intelligence tied to specific indicators of compromise (IOCs) or indicators of attack (IOAs) must be applied across backup data before restoration begins. Without clear visibility into backup integrity, organizations risk reintroducing threats, extending outages, and compounding business disruption.

𝙒𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙩𝙤 𝙙𝙤 𝙣𝙚𝙭𝙩? ...

items.title: The year is 2027. You open an app, tap a button, and a satellite redirects actual sunlight to your b...
items.content:
The year is 2027. You open an app, tap a button, and a satellite redirects actual sunlight to your backyard.

This is Reflect Orbital's CEO pitching the tech at UP.Summit in October. Ordering sunlight from space in 30 seconds.

The tech: mirrors in orbit that rotate to reflect sunlight down to a specific spot on Earth after dark.

The price: "a couple thousand dollars an hour".

Their demo video got 55M views. 264,000 applications from 157 countries. 

Indiana and India both asked for the same thing: better light at night to feel safe walking home.

Now they've filed with the FCC to launch their first satellite by this summer. Sequoia-backed, $28M+ raised, two demo satellites confirmed this year. 

The plan scales to 50,000 mirror satellites by 2035.

But astronomers are fighting it. 

The Royal Astronomical Society and American Astronomical Society both formally opposed, saying it would "permanently scar" the night sky.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: The Rundown AI covers Reflect Orbital's plan to redirect sunlight from space using mirror satellites, Sequoia-backed with first satellite launch planned this summer.
items.reasoning: Not AI-related. Space/satellite technology story.
items.summary_html: The Rundown AI covers Reflect Orbital's plan to redirect sunlight from space using mirror satellites, Sequoia-backed with first satellite launch planned this summer.
items.content_html:
The year is 2027. You open an app, tap a button, and a satellite redirects actual sunlight to your backyard.

This is Reflect Orbital's CEO pitching the tech at UP.Summit in October. Ordering sunlight from space in 30 seconds.

The tech: mirrors in orbit that rotate to reflect sunlight down to a specific spot on Earth after dark.

The price: "a couple thousand dollars an hour".

Their demo video got 55M views. 264,000 applications from 157 countries.

Indiana and India both asked for the same thing: better light at night to feel safe walking home.

Now they've filed with the FCC to launch their first satellite by this summer. Sequoia-backed, $28M+ raised, two demo satellites confirmed this year.

The plan scales to 50,000 mirror satellites by 2035.

But astronomers are fighting it.

The Royal Astronomical Society and American Astronomical Society both formally opposed, saying it would "permanently scar" the night sky.

items.content:
𝗗𝗮𝘆 𝟰 𝗼𝗳 #RSAC2026 𝗶𝘀 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 — the world's largest and most influential annual cybersecurity conference. 

𝗧𝗼𝗱𝗮𝘆: Last chance to see @Commvault and the ResOps Rumble at @OneRSAC 

𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗲: "Resilience and Operations, The Ultimate Tag Team"

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁: Witness ResOps, the ultimate tag team, in action both in the ring and in live demos. See how unified resilience stands up to today's biggest threats: Misconfig Misfit, the Agentic Anomaly, and the Identity Impostor. Real scenarios demonstrating what's possible when resilience and operations work as one. And don't forget to attend the ResOps Rumble After-Party with Commvault.

🟣@RSAC 2026 Event Page: https://t.co/ssOy8srwUi

🟣Visit #Commvault at Booth S-0634 in the Moscone Center (for demos, chats, and the ResOps Rumble)

🟣Book a Meeting with them: https://t.co/ckWMRuU8Bu

#RSAC #PowerOfCommunity #ResOps #ResOpsRumble #IdentityResilience #CyberResilience #sponsored

items.title: Exciting! The latest Commvault newsletter "The Readiverse Insider" is out. Four topics are explored ...
items.content:
Exciting! The latest Commvault newsletter "The Readiverse Insider" is out. Four topics are explored in this new edition: https://t.co/TqKd0VJP0X

(1) Get Your Clouds Together — 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆: In a cloud-first world, resilience is the new readiness metric, driven by unified visibility, real-time protection, and the ability to evolve faster than disruption.

(2) The CISO's Guide to Modern Multi-Cloud Resilience — 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆: Modern multi-cloud resilience goes beyond backup. It requires unified visibility, consistent governance, and protection strategies designed for how cloud environments actually operate.

(3) Building Multi-Cloud Recovery That Actually Works — 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆: Recovery must be designed around how applications actually run, aligning teams, vendors, and dependencies before an event, not during one.

(4) Preparing for Your “Bad Day”: A CIO’s Perspective — 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆: Organizations that treat systems, data, and recovery workflows as interconnected — and rehearse accordingly — will respond faster and more effectively when disruption hits.

And finally, see what #Commvault is presenting at #RSAC2026 this week: https://t.co/0xjG5w6dyN

#PowerOfCommunity #RSAC #ResOps #IdentityResilience #CyberResilience #sponsored

items.title: "X professionals under X" lists are ageist. I've never seen a list of "60 professionals under 60" or...
items.content:
"X professionals under X" lists are ageist. I've never seen a list of "60 professionals under 60" or something like that.

Also, any list of top X people in some domain that is gender/race balanced is sexist/racist.

In my opinion, the most racist and sexist people live in America, and ironically, these are the people who claim to fight racism or sexism by balancing various top lists, whether they be top job candidates, college applicants, Time magazine's people of the year, or space flight teams.

items.title:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world mode...
items.content:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world models:

→ Generate environments auto-regressively, frame by frame
→ React continuously to your actions
→ Maintain environmental consistency for minutes
→ Recall previous interactions inside the world

This means the system is simulating a coherent environment, not just predicting the next frame.

That is a fundamental shift.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Duplicate/near-duplicate of the previous post about world model persistence and interaction.
items.reasoning: Duplicate content.
items.summary_html: Duplicate/near-duplicate of the previous post about world model persistence and interaction.
items.content_html:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world models:

→ Generate environments auto-regressively, frame by frame

→ React continuously to your actions

→ Maintain environmental consistency for minutes

→ Recall previous interactions inside the world

This means the system is simulating a coherent environment, not just predicting the next frame.

That is a fundamental shift.

items.title:
M-Theory! Brane

What if the Big Bang was not the beginning of the birth of how a universe was forme...
items.content:
M-Theory! Brane

What if the Big Bang was not the beginning of the birth of how a universe was formed, but the aftermath of a cosmic collision similar to how this particular universe we are living in formed? In M-theory and brane cosmology, mathematics does not stop at the 3 dimensions we see in spacetime curvature with singularity, but opens the doors to 11 higher dimensions where our entire universe may be just a 3 dimensional brane drifting through a mathematical higher-dimensional reality. As you can see the violent impact between parallel branes could have unleashed the energy that became matter, radiation, and the expanding cosmos itself. The universe was ignited by a catastrophic multidimensional crash of higher mathematical dimensional reality with string theory imposing a minimum possible length and some models allowing pre-Big Bang phases or even repeating cosmic cycle. This is where math stops looking abstract and starts looking like a reality.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 
https://t.co/tZYaFACRVn
